title: Contract Breach and Specific Performance Suit in Bangladesh
metaTitle: Contract Breach & Specific Performance Bangladesh – Civil Suit Guide
metaDescription: When a contract is breached in Bangladesh — remedies explained: damages, specific performance, injunction, and rescission. Civil lawyer guide for businesses and property buyers in Dhaka.
heroIntro: A binding contract that one party refuses to honour creates critical legal consequences in Bangladesh. Whether it's a developer refusing to execute a sale deed, a supplier abandoning a commercial contract, or a business partner walking away from a joint venture agreement — the law provides remedies. Knowing which remedy applies to your situation determines the speed and outcome of your legal action.
toc: Contract Law in Bangladesh: The Basics
What Constitutes a Breach of Contract?
Remedies for Breach of Contract in Bangladesh
Specific Performance: Enforcing the Contract Itself
When Can Specific Performance Be Refused?
How to File a Contract Breach Suit in Bangladesh

sections.h2: Contract Law in Bangladesh: The Basics
sections.content:
Contract law in Bangladesh is governed primarily by the Contract Act 1872 — a comprehensive law that defines valid contracts, conditions for enforceability, and consequences of breach. For property-related contracts (sale deeds, lease agreements, development agreements), the Transfer of Property Act 1882 and Specific Relief Act 1877 also apply.

A valid contract requires: offer, acceptance, consideration, capacity of parties, free consent, and lawful object. Any breach of a valid contract entitles the aggrieved party to seek legal remedies. Contact an experienced civil lawyer in Dhaka to assess which remedy applies to your situation.

sections.h2: What Constitutes a Breach of Contract?
sections.content:
A breach occurs when one party fails to fulfil their contractual obligations without a legally recognized excuse. Types of breach:

  • Actual breach: The party has clearly and definitively failed to perform when performance was due.
  • Anticipatory breach: A party communicates (expressly or through conduct) that they will not perform before the performance date arrives.
  • Partial breach: The party performs some but not all of their obligations.
  • Material breach: A breach that goes to the root of the contract, entitling the other party to treat the contract as discharged and sue for remedies.

sections.h2: Remedies for Breach of Contract in Bangladesh
sections.content:
The Contract Act 1872 and Specific Relief Act 1877 provide several remedies for breach:

  1. Damages — monetary compensation for loss suffered (most common remedy).
  2. Specific Performance — court orders the breaching party to perform the contract as agreed.
  3. Injunction — court order preventing the breaching party from doing something that violates the contract.
  4. Rescission — the contract is cancelled, and parties are restored to their original positions.
  5. Quantum Meruit — payment for the reasonable value of work already performed before the breach.

sections.h2: Specific Performance: Enforcing the Contract Itself
sections.content:
Specific performance is a particularly powerful remedy in Bangladesh for property contracts. Under the Specific Relief Act 1877 (Sections 12–30), a court can order a party to actually perform the contract — for example, to execute the registered sale deed for a property.

Specific performance is typically available when:

  • The subject matter is unique — primarily land, buildings, and rare goods that cannot simply be purchased elsewhere.
  • Monetary compensation would be inadequate to remedy the loss.
  • A valid and enforceable contract exists between the parties.
  • The plaintiff was ready and willing to perform their side of the contract.

In Dhaka property disputes, specific performance suits are extremely common — where a developer or seller has taken payment but refuses to execute the deed. A Dhaka civil lawyer experienced in property suits is essential for these cases.

sections.h2: When Can Specific Performance Be Refused?
sections.content:
The court has discretion to refuse specific performance even where it would otherwise be available:

  • Contract is inequitable — terms were grossly unfair to the defendant.
  • Plaintiff has not performed or was not ready to perform their obligations.
  • Contract is uncertain or incomplete in its material terms.
  • Performance would require excessive court supervision.
  • The plaintiff unduly delayed (doctrine of laches) — typically over 3 years from the breach.
  • For contracts involving personal service (courts cannot compel a person to personally serve another).

sections.h2: How to File a Contract Breach Suit in Bangladesh
sections.content:
The general process for filing a contract breach suit:

  1. Send a legal notice: Before filing, send a formal demand notice to the breaching party — this demonstrates good faith and may prompt settlement.
  2. Determine the appropriate court: Value of claim determines whether to file in Assistant Judge Court, Senior Assistant Judge Court, Joint District Judge Court, or District Judge Court.
  3. File the plaint: With supporting documents — the contract, correspondence, evidence of breach, and evidence of your readiness to perform.
  4. Apply for interim relief: Alongside the plaint, apply for a temporary injunction preventing the defendant from disposing of the subject property during the suit.
  5. Trial: Present oral and documentary evidence. The court passes judgment.
  6. Execution: Enforce the decree — either collect damages or compel performance via court bailiff.

Limitation period: Most contract breach suits must be filed within 3 years of the breach. Missing the limitation period is fatal to your claim — act promptly.

faqs.question: Can I claim both damages and specific performance in the same suit?
faqs.answer: Generally no — you must elect one primary remedy. However, you can claim specific performance and in the alternative, damages if specific performance is refused by the court.

faqs.question: How long does a specific performance suit take in Bangladesh?
faqs.answer: Civil suits in Bangladesh can take 3–7 years or longer, depending on the court's workload and complexity of the case. Interim injunctions can provide immediate protection while the case is pending.

faqs.question: Do I need a written contract to sue for breach?
faqs.answer: Not always — oral contracts can be legally binding in Bangladesh for most matters. However, for immovable property transactions above a certain value, a written and registered agreement is required. Proving an oral contract is difficult.

faqs.question: What is the limitation period for a contract breach suit?
faqs.answer: Generally 3 years from the date of breach under the Limitation Act 1908. For specific performance of a contract to sell property, the period is also 3 years from the date the plaintiff first refused the defendant's demand to perform.

faqs.question: Can I get an urgent court order to stop someone from breaching a contract?
faqs.answer: Yes. You can apply for a temporary injunction (under Order 39 CPC) at the same time as filing the suit to prevent the defendant from acting contrary to the contract while the case is pending.
